name: Ibrahim, the friend of Allah, part 2
epithet: The sound heart that out-argued a king and feared no one but his Lord

era.period: The friend of the Most Merciful
era.place: From Babylon, through Haran and Sham, to Egypt
era.anchor: After the fire, the long migration begins

title: Ibrahim, the friend of Allah, part 2: the debate with the king and the migration out
meta_description: Day 11 of 29: Ibrahim's sound heart, the debate that left the tyrant Nimrud speechless, the reasoning of the star, moon and sun at Haran, and the migration that carried him through Sham to Egypt, where Allah Himself protected Sarah and Hajar was given. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets.
keywords: Ibrahim, Abraham, Mufti Menk, stories of the prophets, qalb salim sound heart, Nimrud debate, bring the sun from the west, the star moon and sun, hijrah migration, Sarah and the tyrant, Hajar, khalilullah, Surah al-An'am, Buruja
source_note: Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets, episode 11 (Ibrahim, part 2). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is Mufti Menk's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: Yesterday a young man walked out of a fire as though he had stepped out of a cool garden. Tonight that same calm walks him into the throne room of a king who calls himself a god, out across a whole country that will not believe him, and finally into the house of a tyrant who wants to take his wife. And through every one of these, one thing never so much as trembles: his heart.

This is day eleven of twenty-nine, the second of Ibrahim's four parts. Mufti Menk opens it on the highest possible note: of all the prophets, none was raised nearer to Allah than this one, save Muhammad himself, peace and blessings be upon him. When the Prophet ﷺ was carried up through the seven heavens, it was Ibrahim, peace be upon him, he found in the seventh, the closest to Allah. Watch tonight what kind of heart earns a place like that.

sections.h2: The heart that had no defect

ayah.text: And indeed, among his kind was Abraham,
When he came to his Lord with a sound heart

paras: Before any of tonight's scenes, Mufti Menk pauses on a single phrase the Qur'an uses for Ibrahim, peace be upon him: he came to his Lord with a qalb salim, a sound heart. Not a heart that never felt fear, but a heart with no spiritual defect in it at all, nothing cracked, nothing leased out to anyone besides Allah. That is the secret of everything he does. A boy who could stand alone in a temple of idols and feel no dread of stone is a boy whose heart already belonged, completely, to the One who made the stone.

And that same sound heart is what he kept trying to hand to the people he loved. He warned his father of a Day coming when nothing a person spent a life accumulating will be worth anything: not wealth, not children, not status. Only one thing will be currency on that Day. Allah names it in the very same words: 'The Day when there will not benefit [anyone] wealth or children, but only one who comes to Allah with a sound heart.' Ibrahim, peace be upon him, understood from a young age that the whole of life is a test, and that the closer a servant is brought to Allah, the heavier the test grows. So when problem followed problem for him, he never once read it as Allah turning away. He read it as Allah drawing near. The Prophet ﷺ would later say the same thing plainly: it is precisely when Allah loves a servant that He tests him.

sections.h2: The king who argued because Allah had given him a kingdom

ayah.arabic: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِى حَآجَّ إِبْرَٰهِۦمَ فِى رَبِّهِۦٓ أَنْ ءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُلْكَ إِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِۦمُ رَبِّىَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ قَالَ أَنَا۠ أُحْىِۦ وَأُمِيتُ ۖ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِۦمُ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْتِى بِٱلشَّمْسِ مِنَ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ ٱلْمَغْرِبِ فَبُهِتَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَرَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ
ayah.text: Have you not considered the one who argued with Abraham about his Lord [merely] because Allāh had given him kingship? When Abraham said, "My Lord is the one who gives life and causes death," he said, "I give life and cause death." Abraham said, "Indeed, Allāh brings up the sun from the east, so bring it up from the west." So the disbeliever was overwhelmed [by astonishment], and Allāh does not guide the wrongdoing people.

paras: Word reached the king. This was Nimrud, a ruler Allah had handed real power and a long reign, and the people of that land worshipped him as readily as they worshipped their idols. Mufti Menk catches the irony the ayah itself plants: he only dared to debate Ibrahim because Allah had given him a kingdom. Strip away the throne and there is no argument left, only a man. So the king summoned the young man who had broken the gods and asked him: who is this Lord of yours?

Ibrahim, peace be upon him, answered cleanly: my Lord is the One who gives life and gives death. The king thought he was speaking to a boy with no mind, and reached for a cheap trick: he had two condemned prisoners brought in, spared one, executed the other, and announced, see, I too give life and death. Ibrahim could have unpicked the lie, but Mufti Menk notes the wisdom in what he did instead: there is no use wrestling a fool on his own crooked ground. He simply lifted the argument to a place the king could not follow. Allah brings the sun up from the east, he said. Bring it from the west. And there the verse ends the contest in one word: fa-buhita, the disbeliever was struck dumb. No answer. A youth, with nothing but a sound heart and the truth, had left a self-proclaimed god speechless in his own court. And Allah seals it: He does not guide a wrongdoing people.

sections.h2: Stars that set, and a Lord who does not

ayah.arabic: فَلَمَّا جَنَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلَّيْلُ رَءَا كَوْكَبًا ۖ قَالَ هَٰذَا رَبِّى ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَ قَالَ لَآ أُحِبُّ ٱلْءَافِلِينَ
ayah.text: So when the night covered him [with darkness], he saw a star. He said, "This is my lord." But when it set, he said, "I like not those that set [i.e., disappear]."

ayah.arabic: فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلْقَمَرَ بَازِغًا قَالَ هَٰذَا رَبِّى ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَ قَالَ لَئِن لَّمْ يَهْدِنِى رَبِّى لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ
ayah.text: And when he saw the moon rising, he said, "This is my lord." But when it set, he said, "Unless my Lord guides me, I will surely be among the people gone astray."

ayah.arabic: فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً قَالَ هَٰذَا رَبِّى هَٰذَآ أَكْبَرُ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَتْ قَالَ يَٰقَوْمِ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ
ayah.text: And when he saw the sun rising, he said, "This is my lord; this is greater." But when it set, he said, "O my people, indeed I am free from what you associate with Allāh.

ayah.arabic: إِنِّى وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّذِى فَطَرَ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ
ayah.text: Indeed, I have turned my face [i.e., self] toward He who created the heavens and the earth, inclining toward truth, and I am not of those who associate others with Allāh."

paras: They had warned him they would stone him, so Ibrahim, peace be upon him, left. Only two souls in the whole land had believed him: his nephew Lut, peace be upon him, and his wife Sarah, peace be upon her. He gave up his family, his city, everything, and Allah guided him north toward the blessed land of Sham, the region that today holds Palestine, Syria, Lebanon and Jordan. On the way he stopped at a place called Haran, and could not help himself: even passing through, he had to call its people to Allah. What he found there was worse than idols of stone. These people worshipped the stars, and stranger still, when they prayed to the stars they seemed to get results. Mufti Menk pauses to warn us: a forbidden thing producing a result does not make it right, the result is the test. What is wrong stays wrong even when it appears to work.

So Ibrahim, peace be upon him, met them in their own language. As night fell and a bright star, some say the planet Venus, came up, he said, 'This is my lord.' They warmed to him: one of us at last. But when it set, he let it go: I do not love things that disappear. Then the moon rose, larger, and he said it again, and watched it sink too. Then the sun climbed, the biggest light of all, and he said, this is my lord, this is greater, and they were sure they had won him. And then the sun set as well. Mufti Menk leans on Ibn Kathir's reading here, because it matters: this was never Ibrahim doubting. His heart was sound from the first. He was building a ladder of argument in front of their eyes, rung by rung, until there was nothing left in the sky to point at. Then he turned and declared his freedom from all of it: 'O my people, indeed I am free from what you associate with Allah. Indeed, I have turned my face toward He who created the heavens and the earth, inclining toward truth, and I am not of those who associate others with Allah.' Notice the word he reaches for again and again, the one no one can dispute: not the stone, not the star, but the One who created. Whoever made me, He is the only One I will ever put my forehead on the ground for.

sections.h2: Which of us, then, deserves to feel safe?

ayah.arabic: وَكَيْفَ أَخَافُ مَآ أَشْرَكْتُمْ وَلَا تَخَافُونَ أَنَّكُمْ أَشْرَكْتُم بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطَٰنًا ۚ فَأَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ أَحَقُّ بِٱلْأَمْنِ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ
ayah.text: And how should I fear what you associate while you do not fear that you have associated with Allāh that for which He has not sent down to you any authority? So which of the two parties has more right to security, if you should know?"

ayah.arabic: ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوٓا۟ إِيمَٰنَهُم بِظُلْمٍ أُو۟لَٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْأَمْنُ وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ
ayah.text: They who believe and do not mix their belief with injustice - those will have security, and they are [rightly] guided.

ayah.arabic: وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنَآ ءَاتَيْنَٰهَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ ۚ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَٰتٍ مَّن نَّشَآءُ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ
ayah.text: And that was Our [conclusive] argument which We gave Abraham against his people. We raise by degrees whom We will. Indeed, your Lord is Wise and Knowing.

paras: His people closed in to argue, and he did not flinch. How can you debate me about Allah, he asked, when He has already guided me? I do not fear these things you set up beside Him. Then came the question Mufti Menk calls the most powerful of all, a young man's question that turns the whole fear of the room inside out: how can I be afraid of your false gods, when you are not afraid of having given Allah partners He never authorised? So which of us, he asks, has more right to feel safe, the one who worships only his Maker, or the one who has scattered his worship among stones and stars?

The Qur'an answers his question for him in the next breath: those who believe and do not stain their faith with the great injustice of shirk, they are the ones who get security, and they are the rightly guided. This is the moment to remember that Ibrahim, peace be upon him, is the prophet who debated the most: his father, his town, the king, the people of Haran, and he overpowered every one of them. Allah Himself calls it His gift: 'And that was Our conclusive argument which We gave Abraham against his people. We raise by degrees whom We will.' It is a quiet warning too. When a person has been beaten in argument and still refuses the truth, Mufti Menk observes, all they have left is force, the fist, the threat, the fire. Reaching for violence is the surest sign the argument is already lost.

sections.h2: A word he left behind in his children

ayah.arabic: وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّنِى بَرَآءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ
ayah.text: And [mention, O Muḥammad], when Abraham said to his father and his people, "Indeed, I am disassociated from that which you worship
Except for He who created me; and indeed, He will guide me."

ayah.arabic: وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةًۢ بَاقِيَةً فِى عَقِبِهِۦ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ
ayah.text: And he made it a word remaining among his descendants that they might return [to it].

paras: When Ibrahim, peace be upon him, said to his father and his people, 'Indeed, I am disassociated from that which you worship, except for He who created me; and indeed, He will guide me,' Allah did something with those words that He did not do for everyone. He preserved them. 'And he made it a word remaining among his descendants that they might return to it.' What was the word? Mufti Menk says it plainly: la ilaha illa Allah. There is none worthy of worship except the One who made you. Ibrahim did not just believe it and die; he planted it in his line, and Allah kept it growing there long after he was gone.

Sit with how far that word travelled. The line of Ibrahim, peace be upon him, runs straight down to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who was sent to a Makkah that had buried his ancestor's tawhid under three hundred and sixty idols, and who cleared them out with the very same word Ibrahim first spoke. This is why the Qur'an calls Islam the millah of Ibrahim, his way, his path. And it reaches you. Every time you say la ilaha illa Allah, you are not reciting a slogan. You are repeating the exact word Allah promised to keep alive in Ibrahim's children, and you are one of those children. The sentence that out-argued a king is the same sentence on your tongue tonight.

sections.h2: The tyrant who could not touch Sarah
paras: When drought struck Sham, Ibrahim, peace be upon him, sent Lut on toward a place that would later be called Sodom, and set out himself with Sarah, peace be upon her, toward Egypt. Egypt was ruled by a tyrant with an ugly habit: any beautiful woman who entered with a husband, he would have the husband killed and take her. And Sarah was strikingly beautiful. Word reached the king, and he sent for them. Here Mufti Menk slows down to handle a delicate report exactly as the Prophet ﷺ framed it. There is an authentic hadith of Abu Hurayrah that Ibrahim, peace be upon him, in his entire life, said only three things that sounded like untruths though none was a real lie: when he said he was 'sick' of the idols, when he said the big idol had done the breaking, and now, when the king's men asked about Sarah and he said, 'she is my sister.' He meant his sister in faith, the only other believer in the land, and he quietly told her so, that their words would not clash. Even here, Mufti Menk pauses on it: this man bent language only to save a life, while so many of us lie all day for nothing at all.

Then comes the part that no human hand controlled. Sarah was taken to the king's chamber, and she did what she had: she turned to Allah. O Allah, she prayed, I believed in You and kept myself pure for my husband, so protect me. Mufti Menk relays that several narrations describe the next moments differently, and is careful to say so rather than harden one into fact: in one, the tyrant's hand seized up the instant he reached for her, freed only when she prayed for him, then seized again, and a third time; in another it was his legs that gave way; in the version Mufti Menk considers strongest, the man simply fell asleep and saw in a dream that he must let this woman go or face his own ruin. The conclusion of all three is identical. He could not touch her. He recoiled and said, you have brought me not a woman but a devil, send her away. And the lesson Mufti Menk draws is one to keep: when a person sets out toward a sin and a barrier suddenly drops in the way, the puncture, the bad news, the locked door, that is Allah's mercy reaching in to stop you. Read it as a rescue, not bad luck.

sections.h2: The gift that became a lineage
paras: Shaken, the king sent Sarah back untouched, and with her he sent a gift: a young woman named Hajar. Mufti Menk notes that the common report calls her a servant girl, but he flags a quieter narration that she was no ordinary servant at all, but of noble birth, perhaps the daughter of a nobleman or even the king himself, given in honour. He leans toward dignity here, and tells us why it matters: from Hajar's line would come Muhammad ﷺ, and Allah chose the purest lineage from Adam straight through to him. Hold the report gently, he says, the way the Sunnah teaches, neither inflating it nor reducing this honoured woman to less than she was.

So Ibrahim, peace be upon him, who had walked out of a fire, out of his homeland, out of a king's court and out of a tyrant's reach, now had with him the two women through whom Allah would build nations: Sarah, and Hajar. They prospered in Egypt until envy gathered around them, and rather than fight, he gathered his flocks and his household and turned back to the blessed land near Jerusalem. The man who feared no one but Allah ends this chapter exactly where he began it: with nothing in his hands but trust, and everything that matters still ahead. Tomorrow, in a barren valley, that trust will be asked for the hardest thing of all.

dua.arabic: رَبِّ هَبْ لِى حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِى بِٱلصَّٰلِحِينَ
dua.translit: Rabbi hab li hukman wa alhiqni bis-salihin
dua.en: My Lord, grant me authority and join me with the righteous. (the du'a of Ibrahim, Surah ash-Shu'ara 26:83)

lessons_intro: A whole migration of tests, and one heart that never cracked under any of them. These threads run straight out of Mufti Menk's telling of Ibrahim's second part.

lessons.bold: Guard the heart, not just the deeds.
lessons.text: Ibrahim came to his Lord with a qalb salim, a sound heart with no shirk in it. On the Day that counts, no wealth and no children will help, only the heart you bring. Tend it now.

lessons.bold: Truth does not need a throne.
lessons.text: The king argued only because Allah had given him a kingdom. A young man with nothing but the truth left him speechless. Never measure an argument by the power of the one making it.

lessons.bold: A result does not make a wrong right.
lessons.text: The people of Haran got answers from the stars, and it was still false. When something forbidden seems to work, that is the test, not the proof. What is wrong stays wrong.
When you set out toward a sin and the way is suddenly blocked, Allah is reaching in to stop you. The puncture, the bad news, the closed door: turn back and thank Him.
Allah preserved la ilaha illa Allah in the children of Ibrahim, and you are one of them. The sentence that out-argued a king is the same one on your tongue every day.

faq.q: What does qalb salim, a sound heart, actually mean?
faq.a: It is the heart Allah praises in Ibrahim: 'when he came to his Lord with a sound heart' (Surah as-Saffat 37:84). Mufti Menk explains it is not a heart that never feels fear, but one with no spiritual defect, a heart that has given no share of its worship to anyone besides Allah. The same phrase names the one thing that will benefit a person on the Day of Judgement, when wealth and children no longer can (Surah ash-Shu'ara 26:88-89).

faq.q: Who was the king who debated Ibrahim, and how did Ibrahim win?
faq.a: He is traditionally named Nimrud, a ruler Allah had given great power, whom his people worshipped. He claimed he too could give life and death by sparing one prisoner and killing another. Ibrahim did not argue that point; he raised it higher: Allah brings the sun from the east, so bring it from the west. The Qur'an says the disbeliever was struck dumb (Surah al-Baqarah 2:258). Truth, Mufti Menk notes, does not depend on the power of the one who holds it.

faq.q: Was Ibrahim unsure when he called the star, the moon and the sun his lord?
faq.a: No. Mufti Menk follows Ibn Kathir here: Ibrahim's heart was sound from the beginning. He was not doubting but teaching, building an argument step by step for the star-worshippers of Haran, letting each light set in turn until nothing was left to worship but the One who created them all. He then declared his freedom from all of it (Surah al-An'am 6:78-79).

faq.q: Did Ibrahim really lie about Sarah being his sister?
faq.a: Mufti Menk keeps the framing of the authentic hadith exactly. The Prophet ﷺ mentioned three statements in Ibrahim's life that sounded like lies but were not: the two about the idols, and telling the tyrant's men 'she is my sister.' He meant his sister in faith, his only fellow believer, and told Sarah so. It was said only to protect a life. Allah then protected her directly, and the would-be abuser could not lay a hand on her.

faq.q: How does this connect to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ?
faq.a: Directly, by blood and by message. The word Ibrahim left in his descendants, la ilaha illa Allah, ran down his line to Muhammad ﷺ, who was found nearest to Allah in the seventh heaven beside Ibrahim on the Night Journey, and who is, like Ibrahim, called the khalil, the intimate friend of Allah, in authentic narration. From Hajar's line came the final Prophet ﷺ himself. Islam is called the millah of Ibrahim, his very path, completed in the Prophet ﷺ.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: Look back over everything that happened tonight: a fire survived, a king silenced, a country argued with, a tyrant defeated without a blow, a wife protected by Allah's own hand. And through all of it the man at the centre never panicked, because his heart was whole. That is what Allah honoured in Ibrahim, peace be upon him, above almost every other prophet, and that is the inheritance he left you: a single word, la ilaha illa Allah, that Allah promised to keep alive in his children, and a way of meeting the tests of life as opportunities to draw nearer rather than proof of abandonment.

So ask for what he asked for, and walk the way he walked. O Allah, grant us the sound heart of Ibrahim, free of every hidden partner, fearing none but You. Keep alive on our tongues and in our children the word You kept alive in his. Let us read every hardship as Your nearness and not Your distance, join us to the millah of Ibrahim and to Your final Messenger ﷺ who completed it, and gather us with Your friend and Your Beloved in the home You prepared for those who believe. Ameen.

related.label: The six pillars of iman
related.note: Believing in every prophet is the fourth article of your faith

slides:
Day 11 of 29.

He walked out of a fire as if it were a cool garden. Tonight he walks into a king's court, across a country that won't believe him, and into a tyrant's house.

And his heart never once trembles.

The secret of everything Ibrahim does:

a qalb salim. A sound heart. Not fearless, but with no defect, no share of worship leased to anyone but Allah.

On the Day that counts, it is the only thing that will help you.

The king said: I too give life and death.

Ibrahim didn't argue. He went higher:

'Allah brings the sun from the east. Bring it from the west.'

The Qur'an: 'the disbeliever was struck dumb.' (2:258)

At Haran they worshipped the stars, and got results.

Mufti Menk's warning: a forbidden thing that seems to work is the test, not the proof.

What is wrong stays wrong, even when it works.

Star, then moon, then sun, each one he called 'my lord,' then watched it set.

He wasn't doubting. He was building a ladder, rung by rung, until nothing was left to worship but the One who made them all.

His question that turned the room inside out:

How can I fear your false gods, when you don't fear having given Allah partners?

Which of us, then, has more right to feel safe? (al-An'am 6:81)

Sarah, alone, taken to a tyrant's chamber, prayed: O Allah, I kept myself pure, protect me.

His hand seized up. He could not touch her.

When a barrier drops before a sin, that is Allah's mercy, not bad luck.

Allah kept one word alive in Ibrahim's children: la ilaha illa Allah.

It ran down his line to Muhammad ﷺ, who cleared Makkah's idols with it.

You are one of those children. It is on your tongue tonight.
social.caption: Day 11 of 29: Ibrahim, the friend of Allah, part 2. A sound heart that out-argued a self-proclaimed god, reasoned its way past the star, the moon and the sun, and feared no one but its Maker, even when a tyrant tried to take Sarah and Allah Himself stepped in to protect her. The word he left behind, la ilaha illa Allah, ran straight down his line to the Prophet ﷺ, who is also called Allah's khalil, his intimate friend. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's beloved series (the episode is embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A prophet a day, connected back to the Qur'an and the life of the Prophet ﷺ, at buruja.com. #StoriesOfTheProphets #MuftiMenk #Ibrahim #Abraham #Quran #Buruja #islam #seerah #tawhid #iman

email.subject: He told a king to bring the sun from the west
email.alt_subject: The heart that feared no one but Allah
email.preview: Day 11 of 29: Ibrahim out-argues a god-king, and Allah protects Sarah Himself.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

Yesterday Ibrahim, peace be upon him, walked out of a fire as though it were a cool garden. Today that same calm carries him somewhere harder: into the throne room of a king who calls himself a god, out across a whole country that will not believe him, and finally into the house of a tyrant who wants to take his wife.

And through every one of these, one thing never trembles: his heart. The Qur'an gives it a name, qalb salim, a sound heart (Surah as-Saffat 37:84). Not a heart that never feels fear, Mufti Menk explains, but one with no defect in it, a heart that has given no share of its worship to anyone besides Allah. That is the secret of everything he does.

Watch what it lets him do. When the king Nimrud boasts that he too gives life and death, Ibrahim does not argue the point; he lifts it out of the king's reach: 'Allah brings up the sun from the east, so bring it up from the west.' The verse ends the contest in a word, fa-buhita, the disbeliever was struck dumb (Surah al-Baqarah 2:258). At Haran, where the people worship the stars and seem to get results, he reasons them off the star, the moon and the sun one by one, until nothing is left to worship but the One who created them (Surah al-An'am 6:78-79). And when a tyrant in Egypt has Sarah, peace be upon her, brought to his chamber, she turns to Allah, and the man simply cannot touch her, his hand frozen until he sends her away, and with her, gives the gift who was Hajar.

There is a thread running under all of it, and it reaches you. The word Ibrahim left behind, la ilaha illa Allah, Allah promised to keep alive in his children (Surah az-Zukhruf 43:28). It ran straight down his line to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who cleared Makkah's idols with the very same word, and who is, like Ibrahim, called Allah's khalil, His intimate friend. You are one of those children, and that sentence is on your tongue every single day.

Tomorrow, in a barren valley, Ibrahim's trust will be asked for the hardest thing of all. Tonight, just his du'a: My Lord, grant me sound judgement, and join me with the righteous.
